coredata.dc:title: Localized surface plasmon resonance-induced charge carrier localization at the silver nanoparticle/graphene quantum dot interface
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Young-Jun
Park, Byoung-Nam
coredata.dc:description: We report a novel investigation into the effects of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) on charge carrier localization and dynamics, utilizing a test structure where pentacene is layered over a graphene quantum dot (GQD) film. To induce LSPR, we strategically embedded silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) between the pentacene and GQD films. This configuration led to a notable shift in the threshold voltage when the structure was illuminated at a resonance frequency of 450 nm. This shift is primarily attributed to the LSPR-induced charge transfer occurring at the interface between the Ag NPs and the GQD film. Remarkably, following the discontinuation of illumination, there was a noticeable and gradual release of electrons previously localized at the Ag NP/GQD interface. This phenomenon demonstrated effective retention of electrons at the interface, which subsequently led to an enhancement in the field-effect transistor (FET) mobility. This enhancement in the FET mobility is likely due to the diminished Coulombic interaction between the localized electrons and the mobile hole carriers within the pentacene channel.“
coredata.dcterms:subject: Localized surface plasmon resonance
Charge Transfer
Localization
Field effect transistor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2015R1A6A1A03031833, NRF-2019R1F1A1060042 and NRF-2020R1A2C1007258). This work was also supported by the 2024 Hongik Faculty Research Support Fund.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Plots of optical absorbance between GQD and Ag NPs/GQD films. (b) SEM image of Ag NPs on a glass substrate. (c) AFM amplitude image of GQD and (d) height image of pentacene/GQD.

(a) ID-VG plots on a semilogarithmic scale for pentacene/(Ag)/GQD FETs in the dark and under illumination (650 nm). ID-VG plots for (b) pentacene/GQD FETs and (c) pentacene/Ag NP/GQD in the dark and under illumination (650 nm). Drain voltage was fixed at −5 V.
(a) ID-VG plots for pentacene/Ag NP/GQD over time. (b) Illustration of the mechanism of threshold voltage shift between illumination and dark conditions.

head.ce:title: Localized surface plasmon resonance-induced charge carrier localization at the silver nanoparticle/graphene quantum dot interface

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering Hongik University, 72-1, Sangsu-dong, Mapo-gu, Seoul 121-791, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering Hongik University, 72-1, Sangsu-dong, Mapo-gu, Seoul 121-791, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a novel investigation into the effects of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) on charge carrier localization and dynamics, utilizing a test structure where pentacene is layered over a graphene quantum dot (GQD) film. To induce LSPR, we strategically embedded silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) between the pentacene and GQD films. This configuration led to a notable shift in the threshold voltage when the structure was illuminated at a resonance frequency of 450 nm. This shift is primarily attributed to the LSPR-induced charge transfer occurring at the interface between the Ag NPs and the GQD film. Remarkably, following the discontinuation of illumination, there was a noticeable and gradual release of electrons previously localized at the Ag NP/GQD interface. This phenomenon demonstrated effective retention of electrons at the interface, which subsequently led to an enhancement in the field-effect transistor (FET) mobility. This enhancement in the FET mobility is likely due to the diminished Coulombic interaction between the localized electrons and the mobile hole carriers within the pentacene channel.“

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the manipulation of charge transfer dynamics in semiconductor materials has emerged as a cornerstone in the advancement of optoelectronic devices [1]. Prevailing research in this realm has predominantly focused on traditional methods of charge carrier management, such as doping and field-effect modulation [2]. However, these techniques often encounter limitations in terms of dynamic control and response to external stimuli. A promising alternative that has garnered attention is the exploitation of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [3]. LSPR, primarily observed in metal nanoparticles, offers a pathway to modulate electronic properties through light-matter interactions [4,5,6]. While previous studies have successfully demonstrated LSPR-induced enhancements in photovoltaic and photocatalytic efficiencies, the specific influence of LSPR on charge carrier localization and mobility in organic–inorganic hybrid structures remains inadequately explored [7].

Our research diverges from traditional paradigms by investigating the role of LSPR in altering charge carrier dynamics within a novel test structure comprising pentacene deposited on graphene quantum dot (GQD) films. Notably, silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) embedded between the pentacene and GQD layers serve as the focal point for inducing LSPR. This approach sets itself apart from traditional techniques that predominantly employ spectroscopic methods focused primarily on the interfacial charge transfer phenomena [8]. Our work represents a groundbreaking foray into the realm of semiconductor physics, where we dissect and quantify the phenomena of charge transfer and retention at the Ag NP/GQD interface under LSPR conditions. This study stands at the forefront of research by pioneering the quantification of LSPR-induced charge carrier dynamics within a hybrid material system. We have unveiled how LSPR can significantly modulate field-effect transistor (FET) performance, yielding distinctive current–voltage characteristics that open new avenues for electronic device innovation.

Graphene oxide (GO) sheets, weighing 0.1 g, were subjected to an oxidation process in a mixture of concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 20 mL) and nitric acid (HNO3, 60 mL) for 20 h at room temperature. Following this, the mixture underwent sonication for an additional 20 h. To remove the acids, the resultant mixture was calcined at 350 °C for one hour, and then dispersed in 40 mL of deionized water, forming a black suspension. This suspension was subsequently filtered through a 0.22 µm nylon membrane, yielding a brown solution. To produce graphene quantum dots (GQDs), this solution was dialyzed overnight in a bag with a 2000 Da molecular weight cutoff.

For the fabrication of field-effect transistors (FETs), source and drain electrodes composed of gold/titanium (Au/Ti) were patterned onto a 200 nm silicon dioxide (SiO2) gate dielectric layer, using photolithography techniques. The channel dimensions were set to 10 μm in length and 2 mm in width. A highly-doped p-type silicon substrate was utilized as the gate electrode. The GQD film was applied to this substrate by immersing it in a 0.1 mg/mL GQD solution, followed by transferring it into a vacuum chamber. Finally, pentacene was thermally deposited over the channel region. All electrical measurements of the FETs were conducted in a vacuum environment to prevent exposure to air. The detailed explanations are provided in the Supporting Information.

In Fig. 1 (a), we present the LSPR-induced optical absorption characteristics resulting from the incorporation of Ag NPs. The absorption spectrum reveals a prominent peak at 470 nm, which we attribute to the LSPR effect of the Ag NPs with a size of ∼ 10 nm, as seen in the scanning electron microscopy image in Fig. 1(b). In comparison, control samples without Ag NPs exhibited negligible optical absorption, underscoring the vital role of Ag NPs in enhancing light absorption. Fig. 1(c) presents a tapping mode atomic force microscopy (AFM) amplitude image, illustrating the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on a silicon dioxide (SiO2) substrate. Additionally, Fig. 1(d) showcases an AFM height image, revealing the island-like topography characteristic of the pentacene layer.

We evaluated two configurations of bottom-contact FETs: one with a pentacene/GQD interface and the other incorporating an additional Ag NPs layer between the pentacene and GQD layers, as shown in Fig. 2 . FETs without Ag NPs exhibited an on/off ratio of 104 in Fig. 2(a). However, in the FETs with Ag NPs, no onset voltage was detected within the scanned gate voltage range. A significant difference was observed in the subthreshold swing, a key indicator of interface trap density. In the absence of Ag NPs, the subthreshold swing was estimated to be 3.8 V/dec. In contrast, a considerably higher subthreshold swing was estimated for devices with Ag NPs. This suggests that the incorporation of Ag NPs leads to an increased interface trap density.

The ID-VG curves in linear scale in Fig. 2 demonstrate a significant threshold voltage shift following the deposition of Ag NPs. This shift can be attributed to the creation of interface traps, a consequence of electron localization at the pentacene/GQD interface. After the incorporation of Ag NPs, we observed a threshold voltage shift from −7 to 86 V, and further from −7 to 104 V, in dark conditions and after illumination with 650 nm light, respectively. The threshold voltage shift in the dark is attributed to the formation of interface dipole by inclusion of Ag NPs [9].

Illumination of the pentacene/Ag NPs/GQD FETs at a resonance frequency of 450 nm resulted in a noteworthy shift in the threshold voltage, changing from −7 V (as shown by black dots in Fig. 2(b)) to 109 V (illustrated by blue dots in Fig. 3 (a)), in stark contrast to the control pentacene/GQD FET without Ag NPs, which was measured in the dark. This shift is primarily due to the LSPR-induced electron charge transfer by the Ag NPs at the pentacene/Ag NP interface, consequently resulting in an earlier turn-on of the FET due to the accumulation of holes in the pentacene layer, as illustrated in Fig. 3(b)[ref]. Additionally, illumination at a 450 nm wavelength induces band-to-band transitions in the pentacene layer. These transitions lead to the separation of excitons at the pentacene/GQD interface, further contributing to the localization of electrons.

Significantly, the charge carrier retention of localized electrons at the Ag NP/GQD interface induced by LSPR was maintained for over a week. In other words, after turning off the light (450 nm), the localized electrons at the interface remained stable for over a week. This persistence is demonstrated in Fig. 3(a), where ID-VG curves were measured in the dark over a week. As the days passed, the threshold voltage gradually reverted to its pre-illumination state (black dots), shifting towards more negative values. This trend suggests that the electrons localized at the Ag NP/GQD interface due to LSPR-induced charge transfer were progressively detrapped, leading to this negative shift in the threshold voltage, as illustrated in Fig. 3(b).

Interestingly, after two days (orange dots), this detrapping process of the localized electrons slowed significantly, stabilizing the threshold voltage even after one week. This observation underscores the considerable retention of LSPR-induced electrons at the Ag NP/GQD interface. Simultaneously, we noted a rise in FET mobility over time, corresponding to the slope in the ID-VG curve, suggesting a decline in the Coulombic interaction between the localized electrons and the accumulated holes. As depicted in Fig. 3(b), this decrease in localized electron concentration at the interface by carrier recombination reduces interfacial scattering, thereby boosting the FET mobility.

Our research represents a pioneering investigation into the effects of LSPR on charge carrier dynamics, using a novel structure of pentacene over GQD films with embedded Ag NPs. The presence of Ag NPs significantly shifted the threshold voltage in FETs at a plasmon resonance frequency of 450 nm, due to LSPR-induced electron localization at the pentacene/Ag NP interface and subsequent hole accumulation in the pentacene layer. Notably, these localized electrons at the Ag NP/GQD interface remained stable for over a week post-illumination, leading to a gradual return of the threshold voltage to its initial state and improved FET mobility.

In conclusion, our research successfully quantified the carrier density through the localized electrons induced by LSPR at the functional interface. This highlights the significant role of LSPR in augmenting charge carrier transfer and transport, which are critical for the operation of devices. Our findings underscore the potential of LSPR in improving device efficiency and performance.“.

Young-Jun Kim: Software, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Byoung-Nam Park: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2015R1A6A1A03031833, NRF-2019R1F1A1060042 and NRF-2020R1A2C1007258). This work was also supported by the 2024 Hongik Faculty Research Support Fund.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Charge transfer dynamics from photoexcited semiconductor quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, H.; Yang, Y.; Wu, K.; Lian, T., Charge transfer dynamics from photoexcited semiconductor quantum dots. Annual review of physical chemistry 2016, 67, 259-281.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Derycke, V.; Martel, R.; Appenzeller, J.; Avouris, P., Controlling doping and carrier injection in carbon nanotube transistors. Applied Physics Letters 2002, 80 (15), 2773-2775.
Mayer, K. M.; Hafner, J. H., Localized surface plasmon resonance sensors. Chemical reviews 2011, 111 (6), 3828-3857.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Performance improvement of graphene/silicon photodetectors using high work function metal nanoparticles with plasma effect

RSC advances 2016, 6 (31), 26216-26226.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Noble metal nanoparticles in organic matrix

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Molodtsova, O.; Aristova, I.; Potorochin, D.; Babenkov, S.; Khodos, I.; Molodtsov, S.; Vorokhta, M.; Skala, T.; Aristov, V. Y., Noble metal nanoparticles in organic matrix. Applied Surface Science 2020, 506, 144980.
